topic: UPS systems for home office — power outage prep

turns.content: we had a power outage yesterday. three hours. middle of a workday. i need a UPS for my home office. what should i be looking at

Three hours is a long outage. A UPS alone won't cover that — most consumer and prosumer units give you 15-45 minutes depending on load. But that's usually enough to save your work and shut down cleanly, or ride out shorter outages.

Let me help you size this. What equipment needs to stay powered?

- Desktop or laptop?
- Monitor(s)?
- Networking gear (router, switch, modem)?
- NAS or storage devices?
- Anything else critical?
desktop workstation. draws about 350 watts under load. one monitor. networking gear. a managed switch. the NAS. total draw is probably around 500 watts when everything is running

500W total load. Here's how to think about it:

**For graceful shutdown (15-20 min runtime):**
- **APC Back-UPS Pro 1500VA / 900W** (BR1500GI) — reliable, sine wave output, USB for automatic shutdown signaling. ~$200-250
- Gives you roughly 15-20 minutes at 500W draw

**For riding out short outages (30-45 min):**
- **CyberPower CP1500PFCLCD** — 1500VA/1000W, pure sine wave, good monitoring software. ~$250
- Or step up to **APC Smart-UPS 2200VA** (SMT2200I) — 2200VA/1980W, gives you longer runtime at your load. ~$600

**For extended runtime (1-3 hours):**
- You'd need an external battery pack option. **APC Smart-UPS with extended battery** or **Eaton 5PX** series with EBMs (Extended Battery Modules)
- This gets expensive — $1000+ — but it gives you genuine multi-hour runtime

**Key features to prioritize:**
- **Pure sine wave output** — critical for your workstation's PSU. Simulated sine wave can cause issues with active PFC power supplies
- **USB or network management card** — for automated shutdown if you're not at the desk
- **AVR (Automatic Voltage Regulation)** — handles brownouts and sags without switching to battery

How often do you get outages? If yesterday was a one-off, the 1500VA unit is fine. If it's a regular thing, invest in extended runtime.

it happens maybe three or four times a year. usually short. 10 minutes. yesterday was the exception. but the problem is not just uptime. when the power cuts unexpectedly there is a risk of data corruption on the NAS. i have had one scare already

Data corruption from sudden power loss is the real risk. The NAS is the priority then — everything else can be replaced or rebooted, but corrupted storage is a different category of problem.

**For the NAS specifically:**
- Put it on its own UPS if possible. A smaller **APC Back-UPS 750VA** dedicated to the NAS gives clean shutdown time
- Most NAS devices (Synology, QNAP) have built-in UPS monitoring — they detect the UPS switching to battery via USB and initiate a safe shutdown automatically
- Enable this feature now if you haven't. Connect UPS to NAS via USB, configure the shutdown threshold

**For the full setup, I'd recommend two UPS units:**

1. **Primary — APC Smart-UPS 1500VA (SMT1500I)** for workstation + monitor + switch
   - Pure sine wave, network management capable
   - ~20 min runtime at your desktop load
   - USB to workstation for auto-shutdown

2. **Secondary — APC Back-UPS 750VA (BX750MI)** for NAS only
   - Much longer runtime at NAS-level draw (~80-120W)
   - USB to NAS for safe shutdown signal
   - 40+ minutes of runtime at that load

Two-unit approach means if one UPS fails, you don't lose everything. And the NAS gets priority runtime.

Does your NAS support UPS-triggered shutdown?
synology. yes it supports it. i have never configured it because i kept putting it off. that changes now. the two unit approach makes sense. separation of critical systems. i like that

Separation of critical systems — exactly the right way to think about it. Same principle as network segmentation, applied to power.

For the Synology UPS setup:

1. **Connect UPS to Synology via USB**
2. **Control Panel > Hardware & Power > UPS**
3. Enable "UPS Support"
4. Set the time before shutdown — I'd recommend 5 minutes on battery. Gives you buffer but doesn't drain the UPS completely
5. Enable "safe mode" — Synology will flush writes to disk and park drives before powering down

Test it after setup. Unplug the UPS from wall power with the NAS running. Confirm it shuts down cleanly within your configured window.

**One more thing:** check your NAS filesystem. If you're running Btrfs, it handles unexpected power loss much better than ext4 — built-in checksumming and copy-on-write. If you're still on ext4, consider migrating when you have a maintenance window.
btrfs. i set it up correctly from the start. i am not an animal. the UPS configuration i will do tonight. i should have done this two years ago

Btrfs is the right call. And yes, you should have done this two years ago, but that's true of most infrastructure improvements. They get prioritized after the first incident.

One last recommendation: after you set up both UPS units, add a calendar reminder to test them every 6 months. UPS batteries degrade — typically 3-5 year lifespan. The worst time to discover a dead battery is during the next outage.

APC units will flag a failing battery in the monitoring software, but don't rely solely on that. Pull the plug and verify runtime manually twice a year.
noted. ordered the smart-ups 1500 and the back-ups 750. should arrive by end of week. i will set up the synology shutdown trigger and test both units under load. thanks
Good choices. Once they're in, your home office has one less single point of failure. Let me know if the Synology UPS configuration gives you any trouble.

actually one more question. the workstation PSU is an 850 watt unit. you mentioned simulated sine wave can cause problems with active PFC power supplies. how do i check if my PSU has active PFC before the ups arrives

Almost all modern PSUs rated 500W and above use active PFC. If yours is 850W from any reputable brand — Corsair, Seasonic, EVGA, be quiet! — it's active PFC. You can verify:

1. **Check the model number on the PSU label** — look it up on the manufacturer's site. It will list "Active PFC" or "PFC: Active" in the specs
2. **80 Plus certification** — any 80 Plus Bronze or higher rated PSU uses active PFC. It's essentially required to meet those efficiency standards
3. **Physical check** — active PFC PSUs do NOT have a voltage selector switch (115V/230V) on the back. If there's no switch, it's active PFC with auto-ranging input

Since you ordered the Smart-UPS 1500, you're fine regardless — it outputs pure sine wave. The simulated sine wave issue only applies to cheaper UPS models like the basic Back-UPS line. The Back-UPS 750 you ordered for the NAS is simulated sine wave, but NAS power supplies are typically small and tolerant of it.

no voltage selector switch on the back. so active PFC. good to know. the nas draws maybe 80 watts so the simulated sine wave on the smaller unit should not matter. the important thing is that it signals the shutdown cleanly

Correct on both counts. Active PFC confirmed by the absence of the switch, and 80W is well within the tolerance range for simulated sine wave. The NAS PSU won't care.

The shutdown signal is what matters for the NAS unit. As long as the USB connection is solid and the Synology recognizes the UPS, the power quality is secondary. The whole point of that unit is buying time for a clean shutdown, not running the NAS indefinitely.

When the units arrive, set up the workstation UPS first. It's the simpler one — plug in, connect USB, verify the monitoring software sees it. Then tackle the Synology config.
i will do the synology first actually. that is the one protecting irreplaceable data. the workstation can be rebuilt. the NAS cannot. priorities

You're right. Protect the irreplaceable first. The workstation is just hardware and software — reinstallable. The NAS holds the data that matters.

Synology first, test the shutdown trigger, confirm it works under a real power pull. Then the workstation. Good prioritization.

da. should have done all of this a long time ago. one power outage and suddenly infrastructure becomes a priority. that is always how it works. the incident creates the urgency. anyway. the units arrive thursday. i will set it up over the weekend

That's human nature. Nobody invests in redundancy until they experience the failure it would have prevented. The important thing is you're fixing it now, not after the second incident.

Enjoy the weekend project. Once both units are tested and running, your home office is significantly more resilient.
